coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free fluorescence determination of uric acid and trace Hg(II) in serum using Si/N doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Dan
Luo, Ya-Xiong
Liu, Wei-Ping
Li, Yong-Sheng
Gao, Xiu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescence probe method for the detection of Hg(II) in serum was established, which has the detection limit of 3.57 nM and quantification limit of 5 nM, based on the electrostatic induced agglomeration quenching and complexation between Hg(II) and silicon-nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Si/N-CDs). Furthermore, the fluorescence probe also showed the satisfactory results in the determination of Hg(II) in human serum. Subsequently, take advantage of the uric acid (UA) to recover the fluorescence of the Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) complex probe, another enzyme-free ways to determine UA was developed. The complex probe can selectively detect the UA content in the 0.5–30 μM range, and its detection limit can reach 0.14 μM, which has successfully detected the UA in total serum, and the results were no significant difference comparing with the controls.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme-free
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Uric acid
Hg(II)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination procedure of Hg(II) and UA
Effects of pH and ionic strength as well as light on Si/N-CDs fluorescence
Sensing mechanisms of the CDs for Hg(II) and UA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of Si/N-CDs and the detection schematic diagram of Hg(II) and uric acid.
Molecule spectra of Si/N-CDs. (a) Spectra of the CDs (inset: photo of Si/N-CDs solution in sunlight and UV light); (b) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs.

Characterization of Si/N-CDs. (a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs; (b) XPS spectrum of the CDs; (c) C (1s) XPS spectrum, (d) N (1s) XPS spectrum, (e) O (1Ss) XPS spectrum, and (f) Si (2p) XPS spectrum of Si/N-CDs.

Sensing mechanisms of the CDs for Hg(II) and UA. (a) Molecule spectra; (b) TEM image and particle size of Si/N-CDs; (c) TEM image and particle size of Si/N-CDs-Hg(II); (d) Effect of different-concentration Hg(II) on the absorbance of UA at 293 nm.

Effect of Hg(II) on fluorescence of Si/N-CDs. (a) Effect of Hg(II), from top to bottom: 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20 μM Hg(II); (b) Relationship between ΔF Q and Hg(II) in 0.005–0.1 μM and 0.1–6 μM range.

Effects of UA on fluorescence of the composite probe. (a) Effect of UA concentration. From bottom to top were 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 μM UA; (b) Relationship between ΔFE and UA contents (0.5–8 μM and 8–30 μM), in which the square-dot and circle-dot curves were UA standard curves without and with activated carbon treatment, respectively.

Influences of coexisting interfering substances and the elimination of interference. (a) Effect of the same concentration (20 μM) of molecules on the fluorescence intensity of Si/N-CDs-Hg(II); (b) Effects of GSH, AsA and UA after pretreating with different amounts of the activated carbon on Si/N-CDs-Hg(II); (c) Effects of GSH, AsA and UA after pretreating under different oxidation time of actived carbon on Si/N-CDs-Hg(II).

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free fluorescence determination of uric acid and trace Hg(II) in serum using Si/N doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People’s Hospital, Zigong 643000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People’s Hospital, Zigong 643000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescence probe method for the detection of Hg(II) in serum was established, which has the detection limit of 3.57 nM and quantification limit of 5 nM, based on the electrostatic induced agglomeration quenching and complexation between Hg(II) and silicon-nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Si/N-CDs). Furthermore, the fluorescence probe also showed the satisfactory results in the determination of Hg(II) in human serum. Subsequently, take advantage of the uric acid (UA) to recover the fluorescence of the Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) complex probe, another enzyme-free ways to determine UA was developed. The complex probe can selectively detect the UA content in the 0.5–30 μM range, and its detection limit can reach 0.14 μM, which has successfully detected the UA in total serum, and the results were no significant difference comparing with the controls.

ce:section.ce:para: The uric acid (UA) is one of the end products in purine metabolism and nucleic acid catabolism, existing in serum, and its concentration is in the range of 0.15–0.42 mM [1–3]. Excessive UA will accumulate in the human joints and kidneys, causing the diseases, such as gout and kidney stones, and the level of UA in serums is also closely related to the diagnosis of hypertension, diabetes, and other diseases [1,4–6]. Thus, the accurate determination of the UA content in blood samples is of great importance for the clinical diagnosis.

Currently in the clinic assay, the determination of UA is usually the use of colorimetric methods, which contain two natural enzymes, uricase and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [7,8]. Due to the high costs and low stability of natural enzyme, the enzyme-free methods for detecting UA have been researched, such as the electrochemical analysis [9,10], high performance liquid chromatography [11,12], capillary electrophoresis [13,14], etc. However, there are some shortcomings in the methods above, such as, requiring expensive equipment, tedious operation and professional technicians, thus, further development of enzyme-free methods is still necessary.

In recent years, due to the advantages of easy operation, stable fluorescence signal, high sensitivity, and low cost, the carbon-dots (CDs) fluorescence methods have attracted widespread attention of many researchers [15–18]. For example, Qin et al. [19] synthesized a fluorescent CDs and used the internal filter effect (IFE) between the CDs (λex/em: 250/415 nm) and UA (293 nm) to detect UA. Similarly, Amjadi et al. [20] prepared another CDs containing MnO2 (λex/em: 370/430 nm) to assay UA, in which, they also used the IFE between UA and MnO2. But IFE is a physical phenomenon, its selectivity is poor, most organic compounds in serum, such as purine, biological enzymes [21] etc., will interfere with the detection of UA. Additionally, Wu et al. [22] also reported a CDs method, which was based on the redox reaction between UA and the CDs, but strong reducing substances also caused the interference. Therefore, in the enzyme-free fluorescent CDs methods to detect UA, the elimination of coexisting interferences (e.g. ascorbic acid (AsA), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), etc.) are difficult to solve [23] and are the goal to develop new methods for the accurate analysis of UA.

Besides, Hg(II), as a pollutant, seriously endangers human health [24,25], for the sake of human health, many methods to analyze trace Hg(II) have been reported, and the fluorescence method is one of the most popular methods. Owing to the CDs having stable fluorescence signal and tunable emission, it is generally applied in determination of the trace-level Hg(II). Lots of the reports [26–31] indicated that fluorescent CDs can be also used as sensors for measuring Hg(II), and in the currently reported methods, the exploration of Hg(II) detection methods with highly analytical sensitivity is high-profile. Therefore, the development of an ultrasensitive method for detecting trace-level Hg(II) is also a challenge.

Luminescence efficiency is one of the indexes to evaluate the properties of fluorescent CDs. At present, in order to obtain high luminescence efficiency, the doping is currently a useful way [31,32]. Nitrogen element, as a typical dopant, is commonly used to help CDs to form π-π domain [33,34]; silicon is a biocompatible element, and is often grafted on the CDs surface in the form of polysiloxane, which enables the lone electrons of N gather together effectively to obtain CDs with low toxic as well as high quantum yield [35,36]. Therefore, it is essential to obtain a kind of CDs with good biocompatibility and high quantum yield.

So far, there is no the report for sequentially detecting Hg(II) and UA based on the fluorescent CDs and the complex probe. Therefore, the research synthesized a kind of Si/N-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, using citric acid (CA) and 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTEs), detected Hg(II) and UA in actual human serum and realized the non-enzyme determination.

The main reagents used in this study were CA, APTEs, chloride (Hg2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+), Na2HPO4, UA, alanine (Ala), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), valine (Val), glutamate (Glu), methionine (Met), AsA, Cys, GSH, glucose (glu), ethyl orthosilicate, n-propylamine, and activated carbon. All reagents were purchased from Kolon Chemical Reagent Co. (Chengdu, China) and were analytical-reagent grade. The uricase kit was purchased from Fenghui Medical Co. (Shanghai, China). The buffer containing different volumes of CA (10 mM) and Na2HPO4 (20 mM) was abbreviated to CPBs. Ultra-pure water (0.065 μS/cm) was used in all experiments.

A JC202 type electrothermal constant temperature drying oven (Chengshun, China) and 25-mL high temperature reactor (Mojina, China) were used to prepare CDs. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimazu, Japan). UV–visible spectra (UV–vis) were recorded with a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (AOE, China). A Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, US) was used to collect transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by using an Escalab 250XI multifunctional electron imaging spectrometer (Thermo, US). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were collected with an infrared Magna-IR 750 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, Germany). Zeta potentials was determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malven, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a SmartLab 9KW x-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Liquid was removed from samples using an FD-1A-50 freeze-dryer (Jiuping, China). The serum samples were pretreated by using a TGW-16 type high speed centrifuge (Yingtai, China). All experiments used ultra-pure water from an AKL-UP-IV-10-type water purification system (Kangning, China).

The preparation for Si/N-CDs presented in Fig. 1 was modified on the basis of the literature [37]. Firstly, 4 mL APTEs mixed with 10 mL CA solution (0.15 M), then the mixed solution was transferred to a 25-mL Teflon reactor and heated to 180℃ for 6 h. The liquid product was dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 12 h (MW: 1 kDa) until the dialysate produced no fluorescent signal (Fig. S1), to remove other residual reactants. Finally, the dialyzed retentate was freeze-dried to obtain powdery Si/N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination procedure of Hg(II) and UA
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence measurements were performed at pH 7.5 and under selected conditions (λex/em: 370/455 nm; spectral band-width: 3 × 10 nm).

In the measuring of the Hg(II) content, first, 20 μL Si/N-CDs solution (11 g·L−1) was diluted to 2.0 mL, as the first blank, and to measure its fluorescence intensity (F 0). The Hg(II) standard solutions (1.0 mL) with different-concentration were mixed with 20 μL Si/N-CDs and then diluted to 2.0 mL. After the mixture reacted for about 1 min at room temperature, its fluorescence intensity (F 1) was measured. Finally, the content of Hg(II) was quantified according to the degree of fluorescence quenching (ΔFQ = F 0 − F 1). Based on the International Conference on Harmonization guidelines [38], the limit of detection (LOD, 3.3 σ/k) was calculated, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank; k was the slope of the calibration curve. In determining Hg(II) in serum samples, 1.9 mL pretreated serum sample was mixed with 20 μL Si/N-CDs solution (11 g·L−1) and 80 μL CPBs.

In the measuring of the UA content, a composite probe solution of Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) was firstly prepared by mixing 10 mL Hg(II) (100 μM) with 1.0 mL Si/N-CDs (11 g·L−1) and 79 mL CPBs. Then, 2.7 mL composite probe was diluted to 3.0 mL with CPBs and used as the second blank (F 2). Meanwhile, the UA standard solutions (0.15 mL) with different concentrations mixed with 2.7 mL Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) and diluted to 3.0 mL as the test sample. This sample was mixed for 1 min and detected its fluorescence intensity (F 3). The quantification of the UA content was based on the fluorescence enhancement (ΔFE = F 3 − F 2).

The serum samples were from 5 healthy volunteers fasting for 12 h. The medical ethics committee of Sichuan University reviewed and approved the informed consent forms provided by all participants according to ethical requirements.

In order to determine Hg(II) in serums, the test sample including 1.0 mL serum, 2.0 mL nitric acid (65–68%) and 1.0 mL H2O2 (30%) oxidized at 130 °C for 3 h to remove the organic molecules [39], and cooled to room temperature and its pH was adjusted to 7.5 with NaOH solution. Finally, the sample solution was diluted with CPBs to 50 mL. In determining UA, firstly, fresh serums were mixed with an equal volume of 4% trichloroacetic acid (wt%) for 5 min, and the treated serum sample was centrifuged at 16000 rpm for 10 min to remove serum proteins [40]. Next, the 0.5 mL sample supernatant was mixed with 2.0 mL CPBs and 25.0 mg activated carbon. Finally, the mixture preincubated for 2.5 h, and then filtered with qualitative filter paper to use for the measurements.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the Si/N-CDs solution presented the transparent yellow under the sunlight and emits the bright blue fluorescence at exciting light of 365 nm, and had two characteristic absorptions peaks at 238 nm and 338 nm. The absorption peak at 238 nm was from the π-π* (CC) transition in the carbon nucleus of Si/N-CDs, and that at 338 nm was attributed to the n-π* (CO) transition of the surface groups with lone pair electrons [41,42]. The Si/N-CDs emitted the maximum emission at 455 nm upon the excitation at 370 nm, and its relative quantum yield (QY) can increase to 25.6%, which was higher than that (20%) of CDs synthesized with the same materials [37] (Table S1).

Fig. 2b demonstrated the fluorescent spectra of Si/N-CDs. It was worth noting that when the excitation wavelength of the CDs changed, its fluorescence emission was basically unchanged, meaning that Si/N-CDs was excitation independent.

The functional groups on the surface of the Si/N-CDs were characterized with FT-IR shown in Fig. 3 a. The peaks at 3558, 3392, 2929, 1660, 1566, 1392 cm−1 were contributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CH, CO, CN, respectively; the peaks at 1493, 1333, 783, 692 cm−1 were assigned to bending vibrations of OH, NH, CH, CN, respectively [43–46]; the bands ranging from 1034 cm−1 to 1134 cm−1 is pertained to SiOSi stretching vibration [43].

Fig. 3b showed five peaks at 102.3, 116.3, 284.3, 299.3 and 532.3 eV on the full XPS spectrum, corresponding to the binding energies of Si (2p), Si (2s), C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s) respectively, which proved that the surface of synthesized Si/N-CDs indeed include Si, C, N and O, and respectively account for 12% Si, 58% C, 9 % N and 21% O. The extended high-resolution C (1s) XPS spectrum in Fig. 3c displayed that Si/N-CDs possess three peaks at 284.5, 285.1, 287.8 eV, namely, there were CSi/CC (sp 3), CN (sp 3), and CO (sp 2) on the CDs surface [47]. The spectrum for nitrogen element (1s), the Fig. 3d, indicated that the different peaks are corresponded to N-Si (398.7 eV), OCNH (399.3 eV) and NH (400.3 eV), respectively, which confirmed that N was present in the Si/N-CDs. The peak at 530.9 eV in the oxygen (1s) spectrum proved the presence of SiO bonds presented in Fig. 3e, and the peaks at 532.1 eV and 532.9 eV demonstrated the presence of CO and CO bonds, respectively [44,47]. The Fig. 3f exhibited that the peaks at 101.8, 102.3, 102.8 eV in Si (2p) spectrum supporting that there are Si-C, Si-N, and Si-O groups, respectively [47,48]. Thus, the XPS results confirmed that the Si/N-CDs surface was mainly composed of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and ionic strength as well as light on Si/N-CDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment evaluated impacts of pH, ionic strength and UV light on Si/N-CDs fluorescence. The gotten results (Fig. S3a) indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the Si/N-CDs gradually increased with increasing pH in acidic conditions, but was invariable in alkaline solutions. The effects (Fig. S3b) of the solution ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity were investigated by varying NaCl concentration, the obtained results showed that the fluorescence intensity remained stable in 5 M NaCl solution, namely, the Si/N-CDs had excellent salt tolerance. In addition, when the Si/N-CDs solution was continuously irradiation under 365 nm UV light for 4 h (Fig. S3c), the intensity of the CDs fluorescence could also retain about 91%, indicating that the CDs had good anti-photobleaching properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanisms of the CDs for Hg(II) and UA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a manifested that when Hg(II) was added in Si/N-CDs solution, the fluorescence was quenched significantly. Using this phenomenon can detect the Hg(II) content. Then, after addition UA into the Si/N-CDs solution containing Hg(II), it was found that the fluorescence can be recovered, which indicated that Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) can be used to quantify UA. Therefore, in order to further understand the sensing mechanism of Si/N-CDs for Hg(II) and UA, the following experiments were carried out.

Fig. 4b-c indicated effects of Hg(II) on the particle size distribution of the Si/N-CDs, in which the particle size of Si/N-CDs was 4.0 nm and that of Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) was 45 nm. The reason for the particle size enlarging may be that Hg(II) induced the aggregation of Si/N-CDs, resulting in the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the Zeta potential (Fig. S2) also displayed that the charge of Si/N-CDs was decreased obviously after adding Hg(II), which meant that the Hg(II) ions reacted with the negatively charged Si/N-CDs. When Hg(II) electrostatically attracted with the Si/N-CDs, the amino and CO on the Si/N-CDs surface can coordinate with Hg(II) ions to form the complex [32,49]. Therefore, the coordination may lead to the fluorescence quenching.

Besides, according to curve in Fig. 4a, we have known that UA did not fluoresce, and did not affect the fluorescence of Si/N-CDs, but can enhance the fluorescence of Si/N-CDs and Hg(II) mixture solution, which suggested that UA may react with Hg(II). To verify the thought, the kinetic detection for UA solution were conducted when adding different concentrations of Hg(II). Fig. 4d showed that addition of Hg(II) lowered the characteristic absorbance of UA at 293 nm, meaning that Hg(II) indeed reacted with UA. Therefore, the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) by UA should be that the amino or carbonyl groups of UA with Hg(II) cause the strong affinity binds to form Hg(II)-UA [50], resulting in liberation of Hg(II) from the Si/N-CDs surface and the recovery of the Si/N-CDs.

In order to improve the sensitivity of Si/N-CDs to detect Hg(II), the analysis parameters were optimized. The experimental results presented that the Hg(II) sample and 0.11 g·L−1Si/N-CDs were incubated for 1 min in CPBs at pH 7.5 to obtain the strong signal of fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4), and these conditions were used for the quantitative analysis of Hg(II).

Fig. 5 a showed the linear examination, which indicated that the Hg(II) ion in the range of 0.005–20 μM could quench the Si/N-CDs fluorescence. Fig. 5b manifested that Hg(II) concentrations in the ranges of 0.005–0.1 μM and 0.1–6 μM were linearly related to ΔFQ . In addition, by the calculation, the LOD was 3.57 nM in low concentration range, namely, the LOD of the Si/N-CDs probe is low than that of other methods (Table S2).

The selectivity of Si/N-CDs as the fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection was evaluated, in which an equal concentration (10 μM) of 15 other metal ions were used as interference sources, such as Na+, K+ and Mg2+. The tested results showed (Fig. S5a) that the fluorescence of the Si/N-CDs was still quenched selectively by Hg(II), but Cu2+ and Fe3+ had a weak effect. In practical applicability, the coexisting metal ion concentration in serums are different, such as Cu2+ and Fe3+ are respectively about 50 μM and 20 μM [51], thus, using higher concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ again evaluated the selectivity. The obtained results (Fig. S5b) indicated that no significant change in the fluorescence of Si/N-CDs containing Hg(II) was observed in the presence of 100 μM metal ions, in which the added concentrations for Cu2+ and Fe3+ are about 50 μM and 20 μM, respectively. These outcomes obviously described the high selectivity of Si/N-CDs.

To evaluate the feasibility of the Si/N-CDs for Hg(II), the serum samples of added Hg(II) was measured by the standard addition method. As listed data showed (Table S3), the excellent recoveries of 96–106% were attained, which had good relative standard deviations as well (relative standard deviation (RSD) < 5%). All the results proved that the Si/N-CDs probe was reliable for assaying Hg(II) in blood samples.

Firstly, the optimal dosage of Hg(II) was examined for obtaining the good sensitivity of UA detected by Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) composite probe. The results in Fig. 5b described that 10 μM Hg(II) could quench the fluorescence of the Si/N-CDs to reach a platform, so the 10 μM Hg(II) was selected to prepare the Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) probe to detect UA. Subsequently, the further optimized tests showed (Fig. S6) that, when the composite probe solutions were in pH 7.5, at 30℃ and reaction time for 1 min, the fluorescence enhancement signal produced by UA reached the strongest.

And then, the linear range of the composite probe was investigated. Fig. 6 a-b showed that, the fluorescence of the composite probe heightened as the UA concentration increases, in which there were two linear ranges in 0.5–8 μM and 8–30 μM between ΔFE and the UA concentration (the square-dot curve of Fig. 6b). The related LOD was as low as 0.14 μM in low concentration range. Furthermore, compared with several fluorescent probes for detecting UA (Table S4), the composite probe exhibited superior sensitivity and lower cost.

Selectivity of the composite probe to UA was evaluated by 16 other biomolecules. Fig. 7 a indicated that AsA, GSH and Cys also impacted the fluorescence of the composite probe. Thus, in order to accurately determine UA with Si/N-CDs-Hg(II), it needed to eliminate the effects of these reducing substances. By means of diluting (Fig. S7), the interference about 10 μM [52] Cys in serum can be eliminated, but the critical concentrations of AsA and GSH in serum were higher, about 100 μM [53] and 500 μM [54], individually, so interferences of AsA and GSH have to eliminate by other means.

Activated carbon had oxidation and adsorption functions [55], and was selected to eliminate the interfering of AsA and GSH on the Si/N-CDs-Hg(II). The dosage and the reaction time of the activated carbon were investigated. 10 μM AsA, 50 μM GSH and 40 μM UA were respectively added to different amounts of the activated carbon to obtain results shown in Fig. 7b. It can be seen that, 10 g·L−1 activated carbon can eliminate the interference of AsA and GSH. Fig. 7c indicated that when the reaction time is 2.5 h, the effects of AsA and GAH can be eliminated completely. But, Fig. 6b-c revealed that the activated carbon also impacted the ability of UA recovering fluorescence. To reduce this systematic error, the sensitivity of the composite probe was investigated again after pretreating the UA samples with the activated carbon. The circle-dot curve (red) in Fig. 6b showed that UA contents in ranges of 0.5–8 μM were still linear correlation with ΔFE , and the LOD was 0.29 μM. Therefore, all the results indicated that the composite probe can be applied for quantifying UA with reliable sensitivity.

To evaluate the feasibility of the composite probe for practical applications, the contents of UA in human serums have been assayed, and the spiking experiments were also conducted (Table S5). Compared with the enzymatic method [7,8], the t-test based on the present method was satisfied (P > 0.05), indicating that there was no significant difference between the composite probe method and the enzymatic method. Meanwhile, the added recovery was good, which were in the 95–104% range, showing those coexisting substances had not interfere with the UA determination any more. These pleased results proved that the proposed methods are accurate and reliable for the detection of UA in the human serums.

Based on the fluorescence quenching produced by the aggregation and complexation between Si/N-CDs and Hg(II), a simple, highly sensitive and cost-efficient fluorescence probe method for assaying Hg(II) was developed. Then according to the interaction between Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) and UA, a new enzyme-free method was also established, which can be used for determining the UA content in serum. The strategy expects to use for continuously develop a low-cost, sensitive sensors for biological assay.

Dan Cao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ya-Xiong Luo: Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Wei-Ping Liu: . Yong-Sheng Li: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Xiu-Feng Gao: Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120182.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Clin. Invest.

Anal. Biochem.

Mult. Scler.

Nat. Lett.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uric acid as a biomarker and a therapeutic target in diabetes
Can. J. Diabetes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lytvyn, B.A. Perkins, D.Z.I. Cherney, Uric acid as a biomarker and a therapeutic target in diabetes, Can. J. Diabetes. 39 (3) (2015) 239-246. 10.1016/j.jcjd.2014.10.013
